coredata.dc:title: Innovative dopamine-modified carbon quantum dot fluorescence sensor meets affinity chromatography: A breakthrough in Tyrosinase inhibitor screening in food
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Tao
Wang, Weibiao
Wang, Mei
He, Xinmin
Ma, Fen
Zhang, Weiman
Zhang, Shuxian
Wilson, Gidion
Yang, Zhigang
Sa, Yuping
Zhang, Yue
Chen, Guoning
Ma, Xueqin
coredata.dc:description: Tyrosinase (TYR), a polyphenol oxidase crucial for melanin synthesis, plays various biological functions, and its inhibitors are widely used in the cosmetics and food industries for skin-lightening and anti-browning applications. Here, an innovative method that integrates biosensing with affinity chromatography was developed, enabling the rapid and precise identification of TYR inhibitors in food. The biosensing segment used a dopamine-modified carbon quantum dot fluorescence sensor for quick detection of TYR inhibitors in complex food samples. In affinity chromatography, bioactive compounds were accurately captured, separated, and identified. The method was validated using both positive and negative controls and applied to screen for TYR inhibitors in 21 foods. The results revealed Wolfberry significantly inhibits TYR, with 2-O-β-D-Glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AA-2βG) identified as the active compound. Molecular docking demonstrated strong binding between AA-2βG and TYR, and anti-browning tests confirmed its effectiveness. The findings highlight the potential for quickly identifying enzyme inhibitors in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tyrosinase
Carbon quantum dot
Enzyme inhibitor screening
Biosensing
Affinity chromatography

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods
Materials and reagents
Synthesis of dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots
Determination of enzyme activity based on dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots
Preparation of immobilized tyrosinase (TYR@MWCNTs)
Characterization of dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots and immobilized tyrosinase
Tyrosinase inhibitor screening model and evaluation
Foods active ingredient screening
Molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation
Anti-browning effect of AA-2βG on freshly cut apple slices
Results and discussion
Preparation and properties of DA-CDs
Establishment and evaluation of biosensing screening approaches
Preparation and characterization of immobilized Tyrosinase
Validation of an affinity screening model based on immobilized enzymes
Screening of foods sourced tyrosinase inhibitors
Molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation
Anti-browning effect of AA-2βG on freshly cut apple slices
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the open competition mechanism to select the best candidates for key research projects of Ningxia Medical University (XJKF240330, XJKF240324) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (82360684).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental schematic diagram for screening TYR inhibitors integrated DA-CDs of fluorescent biosensing and immobilized enzyme affinity chromatography. In the first biosensor step, DA-CDs, made from CA and DA using an 800 W microwave for 5 min, emit strong blue fluorescence. Their o-diphenol group reacts with TYR to form o-diquinone, quenching the fluorescence and enabling enzyme activity measurement. In the second inhibitory screening step, 21 foods were screened for TYR inhibitors, revealing Wolfberry as a significant inhibitor. In the last affinity chromatography step, AA-2βG was identified as the responsible active compound of Wolfberry with an IC50 of 1.4 mM, showing a notable anti-browning effect. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Development and validation of biosensing screening methods. (A) The TEM image and particle size distribution of DA-CDs reveal that these nanoparticles exhibit a spherical morphology with main diameters ranging from 3 to 9 nm; (B) The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of DA-CDs indicate an optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 320 nm and 450 nm, respectively; (C) AFM images of DA-CDs reveal that these particles exhibit a spherical morphology with varying sizes; (D) The fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs was measured across a pH range of 1 to 14, revealing that the highest fluorescence intensity is observed at pH 7; (E) The fluorescence intensities of varying concentrations of TYR were analyzed to construct a standard curve, thereby validating the capability of DA-CDs for quantitative detection of TYR activity; (F) The fluorescence intensities of various enzymes, including TYR, LZ, NA, HGB, BSA, AchE, CA, β-LS, and HAase, were quantified using a biosensing model; (G) The fluorescence intensities of various concentrations of arbutin were evaluated to validate its suitability as a positive control in biosensing applications; (H) The fluorescence intensities of the positive control drug arbutin and the negative control drugs norepinephrine, avibactam sodium, and argatroban were measured.

Characterization images of MWCNTs and TYR@MWCNTs. Panels (A), (B), and (C) correspond to TEM, XPS and AFM images, respectively. In panel (A), both MWCNTs and TYR@MWCNTs composites exhibit similar curved morphologies. In panel (B), the nitrogen content in TYR@MWCNTs is significantly higher than that in pristine MWCNTs. Panel (C) reveals root mean square roughness (Rq) values of 0.151 Nm for MWCNTs and 0.272 Nm for TYR@MWCNTs, indicates a significant enhancement in surface roughness following the successful immobilization of TYR onto the MWCNTs surface.

Validation of affinity chromatography method utilizing TYR@MWCNTs as stationary phase. Panels (A), (B), (C) and (D) present the HPLC analyses for norepinephrine, avibactam sodium, argatroban, and arbutin, respectively, following affinity chromatography. Panel (E) shows the adsorption rates of the established affinity chromatography technique for the positive control, arbutin, in comparison to the negative controls, norepinephrine, avibactam sodium, and argatroban. The results indicate that the adsorption rate of arbutin is significantly higher than that of the negative controls, thereby validating the selectivity and efficacy of this affinity chromatography approach.

Identification and validation of the TYR inhibitor AA-2βG screened from various food samples. Panels (A) and (B) exhibit the principles and outcomes of screening for TYR inhibitors in 21 food samples. Specifically, the catechol moiety present on the DA-CDs can be oxidized by TYR to form a quinone structure, resulting in the quenching of the blue fluorescence of DA-CDs. In contrast, the presence of a TYR inhibitor prevents this quenching effect. Upon conducting a screening analysis of 21 food samples, it was observed that Wolfberry exhibits TYR inhibitory activity comparable to that of the positive control compound, arbutin. This suggests the potential presence of active components in Wolfberry that inhibit TYR. Panel (C) displays the HPLC analysis of affinity chromatography using the initial Wolfberry solution, the unabsorbed solution loaded onto TYR@MWCNTs, the elution solution after absorption onto TYR@MWCNTs, and the AA-2βG standard reference. The distinct chromatographic behaviors confirm that AA-2βG acts as a specific inhibitor of TYR. Panels (D) and (E) present the validated mass spectrometry data and the determined IC50 value (1.40 mM) for the identified TYR inhibitor, AA-2βG. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Molecular docking data reveal the interactions between the compounds and TYR. Panels (A) and (B) present the docking diagrams of arbutin and AA-2βG with TYR, respectively, clearly illustrating the interaction of TYR amino acid residues with these inhibitors. Specifically, in the molecular docking analysis of arbutin with TYR, the protein residues involved in hydrophobic interactions include ALA246, LEU9, LEU11, PRO12, MET319, and VAL88, while the docking analysis of AA-2βG with TYR reveals that the protein residues participating in hydrophobic interactions are TYR311 and TRP358.

Molecular dynamic simulation of arbutin and AA-2βG with TYR. (A) illustrate the RMSD of ligand including arbutin and AA-2βG, as well as the protein, TYR; (B) depict the RMSF of the TYR; (C) and (D) represent detailed timeline representations of the interactions of arbutin and AA-2βG with TYR, respectively. In these interaction diagrams, green represents hydrogen bonds, pink represents ionic interactions, purple represents hydrophobic interactions, and blue represents water bridges. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Color changes in fresh-cut apple slices after uniformly coating them with the AA-2βG solution. (A) Schematic diagram of the anti-browning experiment for apple slices; Panel (B) depicts representative images of browning at five selected time points between 0 and 60 min, demonstrating significant browning in apple slices treated with PBS, whereas those treated with Arbutin and AA-2βG exhibited minimal color changes. Panel (C) quantifies these color changes using numerical data processed by ImageJ software, indicating that the inhibitory effect of AA-2βG on food browning is comparable to that of Arbutin.
Docking simulation results between tyrosinase and the ligands.

row.entry: ALA246, LEU9, LEU11, PRO12, MET319, VAL88

head.ce:title: Innovative dopamine-modified carbon quantum dot fluorescence sensor meets affinity chromatography: A breakthrough in Tyrosinase inhibitor screening in food

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Ningxia Medical University, 1160 Shenli Street, Yinchuan 750001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Ningxia Medical University, 1160 Shenli Street, Yinchuan, 750001, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tyrosinase (TYR), a polyphenol oxidase crucial for melanin synthesis, plays various biological functions, and its inhibitors are widely used in the cosmetics and food industries for skin-lightening and anti-browning applications. Here, an innovative method that integrates biosensing with affinity chromatography was developed, enabling the rapid and precise identification of TYR inhibitors in food. The biosensing segment used a dopamine-modified carbon quantum dot fluorescence sensor for quick detection of TYR inhibitors in complex food samples. In affinity chromatography, bioactive compounds were accurately captured, separated, and identified. The method was validated using both positive and negative controls and applied to screen for TYR inhibitors in 21 foods. The results revealed Wolfberry significantly inhibits TYR, with 2-O-β-D-Glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AA-2βG) identified as the active compound. Molecular docking demonstrated strong binding between AA-2βG and TYR, and anti-browning tests confirmed its effectiveness. The findings highlight the potential for quickly identifying enzyme inhibitors in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Tyrosinase (TYR), also referred to as polyphenol oxidase, is a Class III copper-containing oxidase ubiquitously found in bacteria, fungi, plants, and animal tissues (Lai, Harry, Montserrat Soler-Lopez, & Bauke, 2017). TYR exhibits bifunctional catalytic activities: monophenolase and diphenolase. It catalyzes the hydroxylation of monophenols to catechols (monophenolase activity) and the subsequent oxidation of catechols to quinones (diphenolase activity). The subsequent oxidation and polymerization of quinones result in melanin synthesis (Huang et al., 2023). TYR is a key enzyme involved in melanin biosynthesis, and its dysfunction is a significant indicator of abnormal skin pigmentation (Ko et al., 2023). Ultraviolet light exposure leads to the degradation of the extracellular matrix and initiates inflammatory responses, and this process subsequently activates melanocytes to induce TYR-mediated melanin synthesis, which serves to protect the skin (Logesh et al., 2023). The synthesis of melanin is dependent on the concentration of TYR. L-Tyrosine and L-dihydroxyphenylalanine serve not only as substrates and intermediates in the melanin biosynthesis pathway but also function as inducers and positive regulators of melanin production (Cytarska et al., 2024). Simultaneously, the TYR pathway plays a crucial role in the enzymatic browning of food, a significant factor contributing to food spoilage. This process results in a reduction in nutritional value and a deterioration in taste quality, ultimately leading to a shortened shelf life. TYR plays a significant role in the food industry, primarily in the production of food additives and seasonings. It catalyzes the formation of natural pigments such as tea polyphenols and coffee pigments, thereby enriching the color profile of food products. Furthermore, TYR is employed in the production of flavorings and seasonings to enhance the taste and aroma of food. In agriculture, TYR applications focus on plant growth regulation and pest management. By modulating TYR activity within plants, it can influence their growth and developmental processes, ultimately improving crop yield and quality (Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, the development of TYR inhibitors is very important. Currently, kojic acid and arbutin are the main TYR inhibitors in widespread use (Ao et al., 2016; He et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2022). Natural products derived from foods have emerged as a promising approach for the development of TYR inhibitors due to their abundance and cost-effectiveness (Yang et al., 2023). However, the diverse and complex chemical constituents of foods present significant challenges for the effective screening of bioactive ingredients (Sun et al., 2022). Therefore, there is a critical need to develop an efficient and convenient screening methods for TYR inhibitors that are specifically tailored to complex food samples.

The activity of TYR can be detected using various techniques, including colorimetry, UV–Vis spectrophotometry, electrochemical methods, and fluorescence methods (Ackermann et al., 2022; Jin Li, 2022; Nedellec et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022). Among these, fluorescence methods are particularly favored due to their rapidity, sensitivity, and convenience (Chen et al., 2024; Grover et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2022). Traditional fluorescence detection methods, which typically utilize organic fluorescein and precious metal clusters, have certain limitations. In contrast, the carbon quantum dots discussed in this paper offer several advantages, such as low cost, stable properties, non-toxicity, and high yield (Ding et al., 2022). In addition, conventional inhibitor screening methods are often time-consuming, inefficient, and expensive. Recently, inhibitor screening methods based on “protein-ligand” interactions have been developed, demonstrating significant efficacy and efficiency in the identification of potential bioactive compounds in foods (Contaldo et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2022). Enzymes employed in these screening processes are typically immobilized on a variety of carrier materials, such as hollow fiber tubes, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), silica, and magnetic nanoparticles (Dyal et al., 2003; Guo et al., 2017; Meng et al., 2023; Pota et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2024). Among these, MWCNTs are particularly advantageous as carrier materials due to their lightweight nature, stable chemical properties, large cavity area, ease of modification, good biocompatibility, and extensive surface area. The immobilization of alpha-glucosidase on MWCNTs has been employed for the screening of enzyme inhibitors from complex food samples, illustrating the feasibility of using enzyme-immobilized MWCNTs as a model for inhibitor screening in such contexts (Dong Liu et al., 2024).

This study presents an analytical method that merges the benefits of biosensing with affinity chromatography to support concurrent bioactivity screening, affinity-based compound capture, and LC-MS identification (Chen et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2022). During the biosensing process, TYR effectively quenched the fluorescence of dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots (DA-CDs) (Luo et al., 2022), enabling the rapid and straightforward identification of active samples. Subsequently, MWCNTs-based affinity chromatography was employed to screen and identify bioactive compounds in food extracts with potential TYR inhibitory activity, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Overall, the developed method offers an effective strategy for the identification and screening of novel TYR inhibitors in food sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: TYR, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride, N-hydroxysuccinimide, arbutin, dopamine hydrochloride, and citric acid were obtained from Energy Chemical, Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). MWCNTs were supplied by Macherey-Nagel. AA-2βG was sourced from Alfa Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Acetonitrile (chromatographic grade) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and methanol (chromatographic grade) was procured from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Twenty-one different kinds of foods, including Cucumber, Grapes, Wolfberry, Tomato, Pomegranate, Potato, Persimmon, Kiwi, Leek, Coriander, Green pepper, Red pepper, Bok choy, White radish, Apple, Citrus, Longan, Blueberry, Carrot, Pepino melon, and Cherry tomato were purchased from the local market. Other analytical reagents were of analytical grade obtained from local suppliers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: DA-CDs were prepared following a previously reported method (Luo et al., 2022). Specifically, approximately 0.8 g of citric acid (CA) and 0.3 g of dopamine hydrochloride (DA) were dissolved in 20.0 mL of water to form a homogeneous solution. The solution was then heated in an 800 W microwave oven for 5 min. An appropriate 5 mL water was added to dissolve the resulting mixture, and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 using 1.0 M NaOH. After filtration through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, the solution was stored in a refrigerator at −20 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of enzyme activity based on dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized DA-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence. The o-diphenol moiety present on these carbon quantum dots reacts with TYR to produce o-diquinone, a process that results in the quenching of the carbon quantum dots' fluorescence. This quenching mechanism facilitates the quantification of enzyme activity. To optimize the fluorescence intensity, the synthesized DA-CDs were diluted in PBS (pH 7.4), at ratios ranging from 1:1 to 1:100 to determine the optimal dilution ratio. A 100 μL TYR solution (10–100 μg/mL), was added to an EP tube, followed by the addition of 700 μL of the diluted DA-CDs solution, resulting in a total volume of 800 μL. The mixture was thoroughly homogenized and incubated at 25 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the peak fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI FL-7100) with an excitation wavelength (λex) of 320 nm and an emission wavelength (λem) of 450 nm. The slit bandwidth for both excitation and emission were set to 10 nm, with a voltage setting of 400 V and a scan rate of 2400 nm/min. This experimental protocol was designed to evaluate the activity of TYR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of immobilized tyrosinase (TYR@MWCNTs)
ce:section.ce:para: To immobilize TYR, 20 mg of MWCNTs were activated using 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) solution in 0.1 M 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer for 1 h. Subsequently, 2 mg of TYR was added to the reaction system and allowed to interact for 2 h. The TYR-functionalized MWCNTs were then washed 3 times with water, collected, and stored at −20 °C for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of dopamine-modified carbon quantum dots and immobilized tyrosinase
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies of MWCNTs, TYR@MWCNTs, and DA-CDs were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Talos F200x). The chemical compositions of these materials were characterized utilizing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). To evaluate thermal stability and reactivity, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were performed on MWCNTs and TYR@MWCNTs using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (TG-DSC). Additionally, atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension EDGE) was utilized to further investigate the surface morphology and physicochemical properties of MWCNTs, TYR@MWCNTs, and DA-CDs. Finally, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of DA-CDs and DA + CA mixture were obtained using a BRUKE Vertex 70 spectrometer, with samples prepared by the potassium bromide pellet method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tyrosinase inhibitor screening model and evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the selectivity and efficiency of inhibitor screening, the developed screening model integrated both the biosensor and affinity chromatography models. Arbutin, a well-known TYR inhibitor, was used as a positive control, while avibactam sodium, norepinephrine, and argatroban served as negative controls for validation. In the biosensor section, a microreactor was employed to detect the activity of TYR inhibitors via fluorescence detection. A 100 μL volume of TYR solution (50 μg/mL) was combined with 700 μL of diluted DA-CDs in an EP tube, mixed thoroughly, and incubated at 25 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, 300 μL of positive (arbutin) or negative control solutions (including averbactam sodium, norepinephrine, and agatroban) solutions at varying concentrations (ranging from 50 μg/mL to 500 μg/mL) were added into the EP tube, followed by mixing and co-incubation for 30 min. After the incubation period, the peak fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI FL-7100) within the excitation wavelength range of 320 nm and emission wavelength range of 375–625 nm to evaluate TYR inhibitory activity and validate the biosensor model. Potential inhibitors were identified and isolated from the samples obtained through the aforementioned biosensor screening process using affinity chromatography. Specifically, TYR@MWCNTs were incubated with 300 μL of a positive control drug, arbutin (500 μg/mL), or with negative control drugs, including averbactam sodium, norepinephrine, and agatroban, at 25 °C for 30 min under agitation. Following incubation, the mixture was centrifuged, and the supernatant was collected as the unadsorbed solution. The bioactive compounds retained by the TYR@MWCNTs were then eluted using 300 μL of a methanol/0.1 % phosphoric acid solution (1:1 v/v) to obtain the adsorbed solution. The initial solutions of both the positive and negative controls, as well as the unadsorbed and adsorbed solutions, were analyzed using HPLC to determine the adsorption rate. This rate was used as a metric to validate the efficacy of the affinity identification model. The value was calculated by comparing the content of the positive or negative drugs in the supernatant of the unadsorbed solution to its content in the initial solution. The adsorption rate was defined as (1- (peak area of the drug in unadsorbed solution/peak area of the drug in initial solution)) × 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Foods active ingredient screening
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty-one food samples were systematically selected as experimental subjects for TYR inhibitor screening in this methodological investigation. The selected foods included Wolfberry, Pomegranate, Grape, Potato, Persimmon, Kiwi, Tomato, Leek, Cucumber, Coriander, Green pepper, Red pepper, Bok choy, White radish, Apple, Citrus, Longan, Blueberry, Carrot, Pepino melon, and Cherry tomato. Specifically, the following fruits were juiced and then dried in an oven: Cucumber, Pomegranate, Apple, Citrus, Blueberry, Carrot, Pepino melon, Cherry tomato, Grape, and Tomato. For the remaining samples, 100 mg of each powdered sample was mixed with 5 mL of anhydrous ethanol for ultrasonic extraction for 30 min. The resulting extracts were then filtered and dried. A 5 mg aliquot of the dried extract was dissolved in 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) to yield a 500 μg/mL solution. Then, 300 μL of each food solution was mixed with 100 μL of TYR solution (50 μg/mL) and incubated for 10 min. Following this, 700 μL of a diluted DA-CDs solution was added, and the mixture was incubated for another 30 min. The fluorescence intensity was measured (λex = 320 nm, λem = 450 nm) to determine TYR inhibitory activity. Further trapping experiments were conducted on samples that exhibited the highest fluorescence enhancement to isolate potentially bioactive compounds. Extracts from these samples (300 μL, 500 μg/mL) were incubated with TYR@MWCNTs at 25 °C for 30 min. Loaded and eluted solutions were prepared according to the protocol described in Section 2.6 “Screening Models and Evaluation of Tyrosinase Inhibitors”. Both solutions were analyzed by HPLC, and potential TYR inhibitors were identified using UHPLC-MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the binding mode between TYR inhibitors and the TYR enzyme, molecular docking studies were conducted. The crystal structure of TYR was retrieved from the RCSB PDB database (PDB ID: 2Y9X). The Protein Preparation Wizard module in Schrödinger software was employed to preprocess the protein, regenerate the states of native ligands, optimize hydrogen bond assignments, perform energy minimization, and remove water molecules. The 2D sdf structure files of the compounds AA-2βG and Arbutin were processed using the LigPrep module in Schrödinger to generate all possible 3D chiral conformations. First, the SiteMap module in Schrödinger was utilized to predict the optimal binding site. Subsequently, the Receptor Grid Generation module was used to define an appropriate enclosing box that accurately encompasses the predicted binding site, thereby identifying the active site of TYR. Molecular docking of the prepared ligand compounds AA-2βG and Arbutin with the active site of TYR was performed using the extra-precision XP docking method. A lower docking score corresponds to a lower binding free energy and higher binding stability between the compound and the protein. The molecular dynamics of the compound-protein complex were simulated using Schrodinger2024 Linux, with the OPLS3e force field. Gaussian 16 W was utilized for the hydrogenation of the compound and the calculation of the RESP potential. The complex was solvated using a three-point solvent model, ensuring a minimum distance of 1.2 nm between the protein and the edges of the water box. To maintain charge neutrality, Na+/Cl− ions were added at a concentration of 0.154 M. Energy minimization was performed using the steepest descent algorithm for 5000 steps. A 100 ps isothermal-isochoric (NVT) ensemble was applied to the solute with a time step of 2 fs, gradually heating the system from 0 K to 300 K, followed by a 100 ps isobaric-isothermal (NPT) equilibration at 300 K and 1 atm. Ultimately, a 200 ns molecular dynamics simulation of the compound-protein complex was performed (Chai et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-browning effect of AA-2βG on freshly cut apple slices
ce:section.ce:para: A sharp knife was used to cut the apple into approximately 4 mm thick slices. The slices were then immersed in distilled water for 10 min. After removal, surface moisture was gently blotted with a dust-free paper towel, and the slices were stored at room temperature for subsequent use. The experimental solutions included PBS buffer (serving as a control), arbutin, and AA-2βG, with arbutin and AA-2βG solutions prepared at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. The apple slices were placed on a white background plate, and 200 μL of each solution was sequentially applied to the surface of the slices using a micropipette. Color changes in the apple samples were recorded at 0, 5, 15, 30, and 60 min using a camera. During image capture, the shutter speed, ISO value, and aperture size were maintained consistently, and a standard white reflector was used for color calibration. The images were analyzed using ImageJ software to evaluate the anti-browning effect by examining changes in the ΔBlue Intensity value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Preparation and properties of DA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image of the prepared DA-CDs is presented in Fig. 2A. The results indicated that the fabricated DA-CDs were spherical particles with main diameters ranging from 3 to 9 nm, demonstrating obvious solution dispersion without any significant aggregation. Meanwhile, the XPS of the DA-CDs presented in Supplementary Fig. S1 indicates the presence and distribution of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in the chemical composition on its surface. FT-IR analysis was conducted to investigate the functional groups present on the surface of the DA-CDs (Supplementary Fig. S2). Notably, the FT-IR spectrum reveals the presence of CH, CO, CC, and CO functional groups on the surface of DA-CDs. In addition, the optical properties of the prepared DA-CDs were evaluated using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 2B, the maximum excitation wavelength (λex) of the DA-CDs was 320 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength (λem) was 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 2C, the image depicts the AFM characterization of the DA-CDs. It is evident from the image that DA-CDs exhibit a spherical morphology and are uniformly dispersed. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S3, the synthesized CA-CDs exhibited varying fluorescence intensities at different dilution factors. The peak fluorescence intensity was observed at a dilution factor of 1:60. Hence, all subsequent experiments involving CA-CDs were conducted at this dilution factor. The stability of DA-CDs was investigated under various experimental conditions, including pH, incubation time, and temperature. As demonstrated in Fig. 2D and Supplementary Fig. S4, under neutral conditions (pH = 7), the DA-CDs solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs incubated with TYR for 30 min at a temperature of 25 °C demonstrated a stable and significant fluorescence quenching effect. Nevertheless, as the acidity or alkalinity of the solution increased, the fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs progressively diminished. Consequently, in subsequent experiments, all measurements of DA-CDs were conducted using a solution at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and evaluation of biosensing screening approaches
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental principle is illustrated in Fig. 1. In the presence of TYR, the DA-CDs, which initially emitted strong blue fluorescence, experienced fluorescence quenching. This quenching phenomenon is primarily attributed to the reaction between the o-diphenol structure on the DA-CDs and TYR, resulting in the formation of o-diquinone, thereby leading to the observed fluorescence quenching of the DA-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2E, the TYR activity was quantitatively evaluated by measuring the maximum fluorescence intensity at λex = 320 nm and λem = 450 nm. A linear correlation was observed between fluorescence intensity and TYR concentration, which can be described by the equation y = −5.83× + 609.72 within the concentration range of 30 μg/mL to 70 μg/mL (15 U/mL to 35 U/mL). The coefficient of determination (R 2) was 0.997, indicating a high degree of correlation. This DA-CDs-based method for detecting TYR activity proved to be feasible, providing reliable data for the determination of TYR activity.

DA-CDs were synthesized from CA and DA, with CA acting as the carbon source and DA serving as the donor of the surface functional groups for DA-CDs. In the presence of TYR, the originally strong blue fluorescence of CA-CDs undergoes fluorescence quenching. The specificity and selectivity of DA-CDs toward TYR were systematically evaluated through a comprehensive analysis of structurally diverse biological macromolecules. These included lysozyme (LZ), neuraminidase (NA), hemoglobin (HGB), bovine serum albumin (BSA), hyaluronidase (HAase), acetylcholinesterase (AchE), carbonic anhydrase (CA), and beta-lactamase (β-LS). As depicted in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensity of the system containing TYR was significantly lower than that of the other macromolecules, which was mainly attributed to the generation of o-diquinone on the surface of DA-CDs. These findings underscore the excellent specificity and selectivity of the DA-CDs-based detection system for TYR.

In the present study, both positive control drugs (arbutin) and negative control drugs (avibactam sodium, norepinephrine, argatroban) were used to verify the feasibility of the fluorescence sensing model. As depicted in Fig. 2G, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution significantly increased with increasing arbutin concentrations, indicating that arbutin effectively inhibited TYR activity. Furthermore, the impact of arbutin on the fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs was assessed, with results (as shown in Supplementary Fig. S5) indicating that arbutin exerts a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs. Meanwhile, as presented in Fig. 2H, the fluorescence intensity in the mixed solutions containing avibactam sodium, norepinephrine, or argatroban was significantly lower than that of the positive control. These outcomes demonstrated that a TYR fluorescence sensing model was successfully established, facilitating the rapid screening of TYR inhibitors via fluorescence intensity detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of immobilized Tyrosinase
ce:section.ce:para: A series of characterization experiments were conducted to investigate the relationship between the performance of MWCNTs and TYR@MWCNTs. TEM was employed to analyze their morphology. As shown in Fig. 3A, the microstructure of TYR@MWCNTs exhibited a curved shape, that closely resembled the basic morphology of the MWCNTs, making it almost impossible to distinguish the TYR enzyme fixed on the surface of the MWCNTs. Consequently, XPS analysis was conducted to ascertain their surface chemical composition, elemental distribution, and variations in elemental composition, as depicted in Fig. 3B. The alteration in the N element within the images corroborated the successful immobilization of TYP onto the surface of MWCNTs. Furthermore, TG-DSC analysis, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S6, indicated alterations in the thermal behavior of MWCNTs and TYR@MWCNTs upon heating, and the encapsulation of the protein in TYR@MWCNTs resulted in a distinct exothermic trend. Meanwhile, AFM images (Fig. 3C) demonstrated a clear difference between TYR and TYR@MWCNTs, which further suggested that TYR were successfully immobilized on the surface of MWCNTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of an affinity screening model based on immobilized enzymes
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a bio-affinity screening model for the isolation and identification of specific TYR inhibitors using immobilized enzymes was established. The aforementioned positive and negative control drugs were also employed to validate the model. Supplementary Fig. S7 presents the HPLC of arbutin in the initial solution, the unabsorbed solution loaded onto TYR@MWCNTs, and the absorbed solution after elution from TYR@MWCNTs. Meanwhile, Fig. 4
A, B, C, and D illustrate the HPLC results of three negative control drugs (norepinephrine, avibactam sodium, and argatroban) and one positive drug (arbutin) in their initial solutions and the unabsorbed solutions after loading onto TYR@MWCNTs. Based on the adsorption rates of the four drugs in Fig. 4E, the adsorption rate of TYR@MWCNTs for arbutin surpasses that of other drugs, suggesting that TYR@MWCNTs exhibits a high adsorption capacity for arbutin. The observed phenomenon is predominantly attributed to the specific adsorption interactions between arbutin and TYR@MWCNTs. In contrast, the relatively low adsorption rate of TYR@MWCNTs for the three negative control drugs can be explained by the presence of numerous pore structures within TYR@MWCNTs. These structures promote the retention of the negative control drugs, leading to non-specific binding interactions between the drugs and TYR@MWCNTs. Furthermore, the weak adsorption of MWCNTs on both positive and negative control drugs, as illustrated in Supplementary Fig. S8, implies a specific adsorption of TYR with its inhibitor. Consequently, the specific adsorption of arbutin to TYR@MWCNTs results in a higher adsorption rate compared to the non-specific binding of the negative control drugs. The findings of the adsorption rate of both positive and negative drugs suggested that the affinity screening model could effectively capture, isolate, and identify TYR inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of foods sourced tyrosinase inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above validation, the established fluorescence sensing model was used to rapidly evaluate the presence of potential TYR inhibitors in 21 different food samples. The screening principle is illustrated in Fig. 5A. When DA-CDs were mixed with TYR, the o-diphenol moiety present on these carbon quantum dots underwent a reaction with TYR, resulting in the formation of o-diquinone. This reaction leads to the quenching of the fluorescence emitted by carbon quantum dots. In the presence of TYR inhibitors within a food sample, the fluorescence of DA-CDs is restored, thereby enabling the detection of TYR inhibitors. The findings depicted in Fig. 5B indicate that the fluorescence intensity of Wolfberry was significantly greater than that of the other food samples. This observation implied a potential inhibitory effect of Wolfberry on TYR activity, indicating the presence of possible TYR inhibitory components in its extract. As a result, TYR@MWCNTs affinity chromatography was performed using Wolfberry extract to screen for potential bioactive compounds. The chromatograms of the initial Wolfberry extract, the unabsorbed solution after loading onto TYR@MWCNT, the absorbed solution after elution from TYR@MWCNTs, and AA-2βG standard reference are presented in Fig. 5C. The findings demonstrated that TYR@MWCNTs effectively captured specific potential compounds. Subsequently, UHPLC-MS was used to identify the compounds, which were further validated through comparison with standard references. As shown in Fig. 5D, the TYR inhibitory component in Wolfberry was finally determined to be AA-2βG. As shown in Fig. 5E, the validation of AA-2βG's activity revealed a significant inhibition of TYR, with an IC50 value of 1.4 mM. In addition, the TYR inhibitory effect of Wolfberry has been reported in other literature; however, the AA-2βG in our study represents the first report of its kind (Shang et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the binding affinity of the selected compounds to TYR proteins, molecular docking and dynamics simulation studies were conducted. Fig. 6 illustrates the interactions of arbutin (Fig. 6A) and AA-2βG (Fig. 6B) with TYR in both three-dimensional and two-dimensional formats, as determined through molecular docking analysis. The docking scores for TYR with arbutin and AA-2βG were − 5.482 and − 5.067 kcal/mol, respectively. Detailed molecular docking information is provided in Table 1 . During docking, TYR interacted with the compounds through various forces, including charged (both negative and positive), hydrophobic, and polar interactions. As depicted in Fig. 7A, the molecular dynamics (MD) simulations revealed that both ligand-protein complexes underwent successful transformations following 200 ns of MD simulation. The root mean square deviation (RMSD) values for the arbutin and AA-2βG ligands exhibited stability throughout the simulation. However, the RMSD values for the TYR protein increased significantly within the time range of 100 to 110 ns, and then tended to stabilize, reaching an equilibrium state after surpassing 100 ns of simulation time. Although the RMSD value of TYR combined with AA-2βG was higher than that of TYR combined with arbutin, the stabilization of the protein implied a binding capacity of AA-2βG with TYR. Root-mean-square fluctuations (RMSF) analysis was systematically conducted to characterize the structural stability of complex binding sites, with detailed computational results visualized in Fig. 7B. The RMSF values for the two compounds displayed nearly identical trajectories throughout the simulation, indicating negligible fluctuations in the binding properties of AA-2βG to the TYR compared with arbutin. Fig. 7C (arbutin) and 7D (AA-2βG) provided a comprehensive analysis of the interactions between compound atoms and TYR protein residues, detailing the types and timelines involved. A comparison of the AA-2βG molecular docking results presented in Fig. 6B with the MD simulation results in Fig. 7D revealed that all residues, especially ASP-357, were consistently identified in both molecular docking and MD simulations. This consistency confirmed the accuracy of the molecular docking results. The comprehensive temporal interaction profile analysis demonstrated that the AA-2βG–ASP-357 binding complex maintained stability throughout over 80 % of the total simulation duration in molecular dynamics studies, confirming ASP-357 as the principal residue governing the primary interaction mechanism with AA-2βG. The results from docking and MD simulations revealed that AA-2βG exhibited a high binding affinity for the active site, suggesting potential efficacy in inhibiting TYR activity. Furthermore, these findings confirmed the reliability of the screening method used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-browning effect of AA-2βG on freshly cut apple slices
ce:section.ce:para: Enzymatic browning is a widely observed natural phenomenon in numerous fruits and vegetables, triggered by mechanical actions such as cutting, peeling, or bruising, as well as exposure to unfavorable environmental conditions. This process involves the oxidation of phenolic compounds, leading to the production of brown and black pigments (Hu et al., 2023; Song et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024), which results in rapid discoloration of fruits and vegetables upon exposure to air. The experiment conducted involved monitoring the browning of freshly cut apple slices immersed in a PBS buffer solution, which served as the control group, as well as in solutions containing either arbutin or AA-2βG, following treatment for 1 h. The anti-browning effects observed on the apple slices are illustrated in Fig. 8 . The results revealed that the color of the control group deepened over time, whereas the color changes in the arbutin and AA-2βG groups were negligible. Consequently, it can be inferred that AA-2βG functions effectively as an anti-browning agent for freshly cut apple slices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: This study integrates fluorescence sensors with affinity chromatography to develop a comprehensive analytical approach for the identification of TYR inhibitors. Initially, among the selected 21 foods, the TYR inhibitory effect of Wolfberry was rapidly identified utilizing the established fluorescence method with synthesized DA-CDs. Subsequently, the active component, AA-2βG, which is responsible for the TYR inhibitory effect in Wolfberry, was captured through TYR immobilization affinity chromatography combined with UPLC-MS. The TYR inhibitory effect of AA-2βG was further verified using an anti-browning experiment on apple slices. In conclusion, the developed model exhibits a rapid response time, high selectivity, and high sensitivity, making it a highly effective tool for the screening of TYR inhibitors. Compared to traditional electrochemical and colorimetric detection methods, the fluorescence sensing method developed in this study is more convenient and efficient. Traditional fluorescence detection methods typically rely on fluorescent probes and noble metal nanoclusters, whereas the DA-CDs used in this study are not only simple to synthesize and cost-effective but also exhibit excellent stability, making large-scale screening of tyrosinase inhibitors feasible. Moreover, the IC50 value of the tyrosinase inhibitor AA-2βG identified in this study was comparable to those reported for other tyrosinase inhibitors. The successful outcomes of this research offer novel opportunities for advancements in biosensing, drug delivery systems, and biomedicine. However, the present study identified only one TYR inhibitor, AA-2βG, in wolfberry extracts from a limited selection of 21 foods. This limitation underscores the need for further research with an expanded and more diverse range of food samples to enhance the robustness and generalizability of the findings. Additionally, the study primarily considered single-type inhibition, assuming all inhibitors function by directly binding to the TYR active site. The potential for screening allosteric inhibitors or covalently modified inhibitors was not addressed. Future research should focus on allosteric enzymes and their binding sites to identify multiple types of TYR inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Tao Gao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Weibiao Wang: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Mei Wang: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Xinmin He: Validation, Data curation. Fen Ma: Methodology, Investigation. Weiman Zhang: Methodology, Data curation. Shuxian Zhang: Validation, Methodology. Gidion Wilson: Software. Zhigang Yang: Validation, Data curation. Yuping Sa: Investigation, Data curation. Yue Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Guoning Chen: Project administration, Conceptualization. Xueqin Ma: Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the open competition mechanism to select the best candidates for key research projects of Ningxia Medical University (XJKF240330, XJKF240324) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (82360684).
